title: Rajesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited & Anr.
keyArguments: The petitioner was terminated following a duly conducted departmental inquiry in accordance with the principles of natural justice, wherein he was given adequate opportunity to present his defense.
The charges of financial misconduct and misappropriation of company funds were established beyond reasonable doubt through documentary evidence and witness testimonies during the inquiry proceedings.
The punishment of dismissal was proportionate to the gravity of the misconduct proved against the petitioner, and no interference was warranted under the limited scope of judicial review.
The petitioner's writ petition was not maintainable as he had an equally efficacious alternative remedy available under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947.
The inquiry officer was not biased, and the petitioner's allegations of procedural irregularities were unsubstantiated and raised belatedly only after the adverse order was passed.
judgmentSummary: The Delhi High Court dismissed the writ petition filed by Rajesh Kumar Sharma challenging his dismissal from service by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited. The Court held that the departmental inquiry was conducted fairly and in accordance with principles of natural justice. The charges of financial misconduct were duly proved, and the punishment of dismissal was found proportionate to the gravity of misconduct. The Court declined to interfere with the order of dismissal, finding no jurisdictional error or violation of fundamental rights.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
Writ Petition (Civil) No. 4871 of 2022

Rajesh Kumar Sharma ... Petitioner
versus
Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited & Anr. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR MISHRA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the order of dismissal dated 03rd August, 2021 passed by the respondent Corporation against the petitioner, who was employed as a Senior Accounts Officer at the respondent's regional office in New Delhi.

2. The petitioner contends that the departmental inquiry conducted against him was vitiated by procedural irregularities, that the inquiry officer was biased, and that the charges of financial misconduct and misappropriation were not proved by cogent evidence. He further contends that the punishment of dismissal is disproportionate to the alleged misconduct.

3. The respondent Corporation submits that the petitioner was served with a charge sheet dated 12th January, 2020, setting out specific charges of misappropriation of funds amounting to Rs. 8,72,000/- over a period of two years. The petitioner was given full opportunity to cross-examine witnesses and to produce documentary evidence in his defense. The Inquiry Officer, after a detailed consideration of the evidence on record, returned a finding of guilt against the petitioner on all charges. The Disciplinary Authority, after independently applying its mind to the inquiry report, passed the order of dismissal.

4. Having heard the learned counsel for both parties and perused the record, this Court is of the considered opinion that the writ petition deserves to be dismissed.

5. The scope of judicial review in departmental proceedings is well-settled. This Court is not sitting in appeal over the findings of the Inquiry Officer or the Disciplinary Authority. Interference is warranted only where there is a violation of the principles of natural justice, no evidence to support the findings, or where the punishment is shockingly disproportionate. None of these conditions are satisfied in the present case.

6. A perusal of the inquiry record reveals that the petitioner was duly served with the charge sheet and was afforded full opportunity to submit his written statement of defense. He was represented by a defense assistant of his choice. He cross-examined the management witnesses and produced documents in his defense. The inquiry officer considered all material on record and returned detailed findings supported by documentary evidence including bank statements, vouchers, and audit reports. The allegation of bias against the inquiry officer is a bald assertion unsupported by any material particulars.

7. This Court finds that the charges of financial misconduct and misappropriation were established on the basis of substantial evidence. It is not the function of this Court to reappreciate evidence and return different findings. Reliance is placed on the decisions of the Supreme Court in Union of India v. P. Gunasekaran, (2015) 2 SCC 610, and B.C. Chaturvedi v. Union of India, (1995) 6 SCC 749, wherein it was categorically held that High Courts exercising jurisdiction under Article 226 cannot act as an appellate court over findings in disciplinary proceedings.

8. The punishment of dismissal, in view of the nature and gravity of the financial misconduct involving misappropriation of public funds, cannot be said to be disproportionate so as to shock the conscience of this Court.

9. Accordingly, the writ petition is dismissed. There is no order as to costs. All pending applications are disposed of.

(SANJAY KUMAR MISHRA)
JUDGE
DELHI HIGH COURT
14th March, 2023
